item.title: Trough to bring rain across Fiji
item.description: A trough of low pressure is expected to bring widespread rain across Fiji until Thursday, with the Fiji Meteorological Service warning that some areas could experience heavy downpours, thunderstorms and localized flooding. In its latest weather bulletin issued at 5:30am today, the National Weather Forecasting Centre in Nadi says occasional rain is expected over most parts of the country, with isol
item.content:encoded:
A trough of low pressure is expected to bring widespread rain across Fiji until Thursday, with the Fiji Meteorological Service warning that some areas could experience heavy downpours, thunderstorms and localized flooding.

In its latest weather bulletin issued at 5:30am today, the National Weather Forecasting Centre in Nadi says occasional rain is expected over most parts of the country, with isolated heavy falls forecast throughout the day.

Moderate east to northeast winds and moderate seas are also expected, while overnight conditions will remain cool.

The weather office says the trough will continue to affect the country through Thursday, with occasional showers and isolated heavy falls forecast for the Northern and Eastern divisions. The Central Division can expect some showers, while conditions in the Western Division are expected to gradually improve with only brief showers.

As the weather system approaches, the Fiji Meteorological Service is warning that periods of heavy rain and thunderstorms may reduce visibility on roads and trigger minor flooding in low-lying and flood-prone areas.

Members of the public are being urged to monitor the latest weather forecasts and warnings, exercise caution while travelling during heavy rain, avoid crossing flooded roads, rivers and creeks, and secure loose outdoor items while making the necessary preparations should weather conditions worsen.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Fiji universities criticised for ignoring coup impacts
item.description: Fiji’s universities have been criticised for failing to study the long-term effects of the country’s repeated political upheavals on the public service and governance, with the Fiji Truth and Reconciliation Commission hearing that a major gap in research has left important national lessons undocumented. Academic Prof Vijay Naidu told the Commission it was “a great shame” that higher education inst
item.content:encoded:
Fiji’s universities have been criticised for failing to study the long-term effects of the country’s repeated political upheavals on the public service and governance, with the Fiji Truth and Reconciliation Commission hearing that a major gap in research has left important national lessons undocumented.

Academic Prof Vijay Naidu told the Commission it was “a great shame” that higher education institutions had not examined how cycles of political instability had affected the quality and performance of Fiji’s public sector.

“It’s a great shame that the universities in Fiji have failed to look at the impacts of these cycles of political instability on the nature, the quality and the function of the public sector.”

Dr Naidu drew particular attention to the University of the South Pacific, saying its once-prominent governance programme had disappeared despite the country’s history of political crises.

“The governance programme was a flagship programme of the university in the early 2000s… Well, the governance programme is gone. It’s finished.”

He questioned what that said about the role of universities in serving Fiji and the wider Pacific.

“What does it tell you about universities serving Fiji and the region when governance is such an important issue?”

Dr Naidu said strengthening democratic institutions required sustained effort and could not be achieved overnight.

“The general democratic institutions and the checks and balances associated with them, together with a supportive democratic culture, have to be built incrementally.”

He warned that every military coup had reversed progress made in democratic governance and institution building.

“The analogy of switching the light on and off doesn’t work. Democracy doesn’t work that way. Each time we have had a military coup, it’s actually taken us backwards.”

Referring to earlier evidence presented by Professor Graham Hassall, Dr Naidu said the damage extended beyond the public service to the broader confidence people had in national institutions.

“The wider implication is that there is a loss of confidence.”

He said understanding the long-term consequences of political instability was essential if Fiji was to rebuild stronger democratic institutions and avoid repeating the mistakes of the past.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Pass the Bill or face national march, FTUC warns
item.description: The Fiji Trades Union Congress has warned it will consider a nationwide march if the long-awaited Employment Relations Bill is delayed again, saying workers have waited long enough for promised reforms. Following an urgent meeting of its National Council on Saturday, FTUC National Secretary Felix Anthony said the union was hopeful the Government would finally honour its commitments.
item.content:encoded:
The Fiji Trades Union Congress has warned it will consider a nationwide march if the long-awaited Employment Relations Bill is delayed again, saying workers have waited long enough for promised reforms.

Following an urgent meeting of its National Council on Saturday, FTUC National Secretary Felix Anthony said the union was hopeful the Government would finally honour its commitments.

“Workers have been waiting on promises for the past three years since the Coalition Government came into power. The Employment Relations Act has been pending for more than 12 years.”

Anthony said Employment Minister Agni Deo Singh had assured the Council that the Employment Relations Bill would be presented during Parliament’s August sitting and that the Government remained committed to enacting the legislation.

The Minister also advised that appointments to the Wages Council would be completed within a month after the expiry of the previous tripartite council and announced that Government would adopt a transparent process to review the National Minimum Wage and National Living Wage, with funding already allocated in the 2026-2027 National Budget.

Anthony said the FTUC welcomed those commitments but stressed workers now expected action rather than more assurances.

“The National Council calls upon the Government to prioritise the passing of this Bill, which is in line with the recommendations made in the ILO Committee of Experts Report.”

He said the union was also still waiting for the Government to fulfil its commitment to restore tripartite representation on the FNPF Board.

Anthony revealed that the National Council had discussed organising a national protest should the legislation face further delays.

“The FTUC Council discussed the possibility of a national march as protest should the Bill continue to languish in Parliament and be delayed further by the Parliamentary Sub-Committee.”

He said the union was calling on the Government to move the legislation forward without further delay.

“We call upon Government to progress the much-awaited legislation that will improve employment relations and workers’ rights in the country.”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Brain drain after coups went beyond Indo-Fijians, TRC told
item.description: The long-term impact of Fiji’s coups extended far beyond the departure of Indo-Fijians, with the country suffering a devastating loss of skilled professionals across all ethnic groups, the Fiji Truth and Reconciliation Commission heard yesterday. Academic Professor Graham Hassall said fear and uncertainty created a moral dilemma for public servants, many of whom felt compelled to remain silent or
item.content:encoded:
The long-term impact of Fiji’s coups extended far beyond the departure of Indo-Fijians, with the country suffering a devastating loss of skilled professionals across all ethnic groups, the Fiji Truth and Reconciliation Commission heard yesterday.

Academic Professor Graham Hassall said fear and uncertainty created a moral dilemma for public servants, many of whom felt compelled to remain silent or comply with unlawful directives simply to protect their families.

“When it rains, you have to put up your umbrella. That was taken by me to mean, ‘I have to feed my family. If I don’t obey that illegal ruling order, I’ll lose my position.’ That was the moral dilemma that every public servant faced.”

Professor Hassall cautioned against assuming that civil servants who remained in government after the coups were unsympathetic to the concerns of Indo-Fijians.

“You cannot presume that, for example, a Fijian who stayed in the public service was against Indo-Fijian concerns. There was a suppression of expressions of solidarity across race for this reason, and everybody just kept quiet.”

He said the resulting brain drain affected the entire country, not just one community.

“The impact of brain drain wasn’t just Indo-Fijians leaving; it was a brain drain on Fijians as well. It was a brain drain that affected the country. It’s not just an exodus of people leaving through the airport.”

Professor Hassall said the scale of the loss became clear when he examined Government Gazettes recording resignations from the public service.

“When I looked at the Government Gazettes, particularly for the years 1987, 1988 and 1989, and saw the number of teachers and medical staff resigning from the service, it really brings home to you the numbers that were involved.”

He said the consequences of the exodus were reflected in international assessments of Fiji’s development.

“The public sector came to be seen as a training ground for the private sector and for migration. Once a graduate gained three to five years’ experience in the Ministry of Finance or Public Works, they migrated to Australia and New Zealand.”

Professor Hassall said international agencies, including the World Bank, found that some infrastructure projects failed not because of a lack of funding, but because Fiji lacked the skilled workforce needed to deliver them.

“The World Bank reported that infrastructure projects failed not because of lack of funds, but because of what they called absorptive capacity. For example, how many engineers had left the Public Works Department.”

He said the loss of experienced professionals had lasting consequences for Fiji’s public service and national development.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Minister Ditoka defends delayed Lautoka swimming pool
item.description: Minister for Foreign Affairs Sakiasi Ditoka has defended the Coalition Government over the unfinished Lautoka swimming pool, saying the project is one of several incomplete developments inherited from the previous administration and that Government has been forced to prioritise more urgent needs. Responding to criticism on social media, Ditoka acknowledged the frustration over the delayed project
item.content:encoded:
Minister for Foreign Affairs Sakiasi Ditoka has defended the Coalition Government over the unfinished Lautoka swimming pool, saying the project is one of several incomplete developments inherited from the previous administration and that Government has been forced to prioritise more urgent needs.

Responding to criticism on social media, Ditoka acknowledged the frustration over the delayed project but said completing the pool had to compete with pressing demands for housing, infrastructure and social services.

“This swimming pool was one of a number of half-finished problems we inherited.”

Ditoka said the Government had already completed several inherited projects, including Ratu Sukuna Park, Govind Park in Ba and Queen Elizabeth Drive from Nasese to the National Stadium.

“We inherited them and got on with completing them.”

He said the Ministry of Housing and Local Government had inherited numerous unfinished developments while also dealing with serious social issues.

“The Ministry of Housing and Local Government… has had quite a collection of inherited problems to sort out.”

Ditoka pointed to the completion of the Lagilagi Housing project, challenges at the Housing Authority high-rise flats in Nabua, including drug-related issues, and the need to improve living conditions in informal settlements as competing priorities.

He said Government had to make difficult decisions on where limited public funds should be directed.

“The Lautoka swimming pool is important and, yes, it should be completed. But when you are weighing that against housing, basic infrastructure and improving the living conditions of some of our most vulnerable people, some things simply have to take priority over others.”

Ditoka said it was easier to criticise from outside Government than to manage competing demands on public finances.

“Those of us actually dealing with the inheritance have to make choices about where limited public money goes first. We won’t always please everyone, but that is the reality of governing.”

Despite the delays, Ditoka insisted the Lautoka swimming pool had not been abandoned.

“We completed Ratu Sukuna Park. We completed Govind Park. We completed Queen Elizabeth Drive. We haven’t forgotten Lautoka’s swimming pool either. We will get there.”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Supermarkets struggle to maintain sugar supplies
item.description: Empty shelves and dwindling supplies of sugar are causing concern among retailers and consumers across parts of Viti Levu, with supermarkets struggling to secure regular deliveries. Maxvalue purchasing officer Sonia Prakash said the supermarket’s warehouse had run out of sugar, with the Fiji Sugar Corporation (FSC) advising of production issues affecting supply. She said FSC had only 25kg retail p
item.content:encoded:
Empty shelves and dwindling supplies of sugar are causing concern among retailers and consumers across parts of Viti Levu, with supermarkets struggling to secure regular deliveries.

Maxvalue purchasing officer Sonia Prakash said the supermarket’s warehouse had run out of sugar, with the Fiji Sugar Corporation (FSC) advising of production issues affecting supply. She said FSC had only 25kg retail packs available when contacted.

RB Patel’s Lami branch has also been affected, with manager Sumal Mohammed confirming shelves had been empty since last week.

To manage limited stocks, the supermarket is restricting purchases to 2kg per customer to prevent bulk buying.

Consumers in Navua have also reported sugar shortages.

Attempts to obtain comments from the Sugar Minister were unsuccessful when this edition went to press.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Green light to import sugar
item.description: BUSINESSES have been given the green light by the Government to import sugar from overseas markets. In an interview in Nadi yesterday Minister of Finance Esrom Immanuel said although there is some sufficient stock in supermarkets, Government has allowed businesses to import sugar.
item.content:encoded:
BUSINESSES have been given the green light by the Government to import sugar from overseas markets.

In an interview in Nadi yesterday Minister of Finance Esrom Immanuel said although there is some sufficient stock in supermarkets, Government has allowed businesses to import sugar.

This follows concerns that some supermarkets across the country had little to no sugar on their shelves.

“We had checked last week. The Ministry of Agriculture and Sugar Industry had mentioned there is some sufficient stock,” Mr Immanuel said.

“But we have allowed businesses, if they can import sugar as well because it will cater for the expected shortage.

“However, on the bright side the 2026 harvesting season has already started and that probably can cater for sugar supply in the next two to three months but for now we have allowed any importer to also import sugar if there is any shortage.”

Meanwhile, Minister for Multi-Ethnic Affairs and Labasa cane farmer Charan Jeath Singh said the country may import sugar from Thailand if farmers were not careful.

“It’s much cheaper to import and sell than what it costs to produce sugar locally,” he said.

“Let’s not kill this industry, don’t listen to bystanders of the industry, let’s make it work.

“Sugar industry is very competitive in the global market. It needs economics of scale to survive.”

Fiji primarily imports sugar from Indonesia, New Zealand, Australia, Malaysia, and Thailand to meet domestic consumer demands and supplement supply when local production falls short of consumption needs.

Attempts to get comments from Fiji Sugar Corporation chief executive officer Bhan Pratap Singh were unsuccessful as he declined the calls made to him yesterday.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Strategy to guide future peacekeeping engagements
item.description: FIJI is preparing its first National Peace Operations Strategy to strengthen and guide the country’s future involvement in international peacekeeping missions. Acting Attorney-General and Minister for Justice Siromi Turaga said the strategy recognised the changing nature of global security challenges, where the boundaries between traditional and non-traditional threats were becoming increasingly u
item.content:encoded:
FIJI is preparing its first National Peace Operations Strategy to strengthen and guide the country’s future involvement in international peacekeeping missions.

Acting Attorney-General and Minister for Justice Siromi Turaga said the strategy recognised the changing nature of global security challenges, where the boundaries between traditional and non-traditional threats were becoming increasingly unclear.

He said the national security strategy had highlighted these emerging challenges, with the new peace operations framework expected to shape the country’s future contributions to global peace efforts.

“Since the first deployment to Lebanon in 1978, more than 15,000 Fijians have served in peacekeeping missions across the world, often in some of the world’s most difficult and dangerous environments,” he said.

He said more than 65 Fijians had lost their lives while serving overseas, reflecting the sacrifices made by those involved in peace operations.

Fiji’s peacekeeping role has also expanded beyond the military, with police officers and corrections personnel contributing to international missions.

Mr Turaga said protecting civilians, especially women and children, remained a key priority for peacekeepers, guided by the principle of human dignity.

While acknowledging pressures on global peacekeeping budgets, he said Fiji would continue deploying personnel when needed.

“Our goal is not simply the absence of conflict, but the creation of conditions where peace can thrive, institutions can endure and future generations can prosper.”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: PM praises civil servants
